symbols: flames engulfing the damned — the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels
contorted naked bodies — the stripping away of all pretense and the exposure of sin
figures looking upward in anguish — the irreversible recognition of divine justice
dark cavernous space — the outer darkness where there is weeping and gnashing of teeth
description: A dramatic painting showing numerous nude and partially clothed figures in violent contortions amid a dark, fiery environment. In the foreground, muscular male figures collapse over each other in a tangled mass. In the middle ground, dark figures attack and torment others amid flames and smoke. At the right, a robed figure in orange-red looks upward with an expression of horror. The scene is chaotic, dark, and filled with anguished movement. The overall tone is murky with flashes of flame illuminating the writhing bodies.
theological_description: This scene of infernal punishment visualizes the biblical warnings about the fate of the unrighteous. Jesus spoke more about hell than any other biblical figure, warning of 'the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels' (Matthew 25:41) and describing it as a place where 'the worms that eat them do not die, and the fire is not quenched' (Mark 9:48). The contorted, suffering figures embody the theological reality that separation from God — the source of all goodness, beauty, and life — results in the total dissolution of human dignity and peace. Revelation describes this as 'the second death' — 'the fiery lake of burning sulfur' (Revelation 21:8). The scene serves as a visual memento of the stakes involved in the human response to God's offer of grace. As the author of Hebrews warns: 'It is a dreadful thing to fall into the hands of the living God' (Hebrews 10:31). Yet the purpose of such imagery is not to terrify but to urge repentance, as Peter writes: 'The Lord is not slow in keeping his promise... instead he is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish, but everyone to come to repentance' (2 Peter 3:9).

scripture_refs.reason: The eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels — the direct subject of the painting
The lake of fire as the second death — the fate of the damned depicted here
The unquenchable fire of Gehenna — Jesus's description of hell
It is a dreadful thing to fall into the hands of the living God — the terror visible in the figures
